meta.title: Understanding ClaimFarm
meta.subtitle: From a farmer's photo to a filed crop-insurance claim
meta.promise: Learn how one photo becomes a filed insurance claim, and explain every step with confidence.
outcomes: Explain what ClaimFarm does and the problem it solves
Trace one photo end to end, webhook to filed claim
Name each agent and the service it uses, and why
Answer a judge's questions about limits and fraud

c-story.name: What ClaimFarm is
c-story.summary: A chat-to-claim autopilot for smallholder crop insurance.

c-problem.name: The problem
c-problem.summary: Fewer than 20% of eligible farmers file; language, evidence, and literacy block them.

c-arch.name: Architecture
c-arch.summary: Signature-verified webhooks funnel every channel into one shared pipeline.

c-journey.name: The request journey
c-journey.summary: process_inbound assesses, corroborates, drafts, saves, and replies; a human then approves.

c-agents.name: The agent pipeline
c-agents.summary: One focused agent per step, each returning structured, checkable output.

c-data.name: Data and state
c-data.summary: Three stores by shape: SQL records, OSS files, DashVector embeddings.

c-tradeoffs.name: Tradeoffs
c-tradeoffs.summary: Honest, scoped limits, each with a named production fix.

t-qwenvl.name: Qwen-VL-Max
t-qwenvl.purpose: Reads the crop photo, returns a structured damage report.
t-qwenvl.tradeoffs: Required by the hackathon; strong multimodal quality.

t-qwenmax.name: Qwen-Max
t-qwenmax.purpose: Drafts the claim and translates replies.
t-qwenmax.tradeoffs: Cheaper than vision rates for text tasks.

t-embed.name: text-embedding-v3
t-embed.purpose: Vectors for similar-claim search and fraud.
t-embed.tradeoffs: One representation serves RAG and fraud.

t-fastapi.name: FastAPI
t-fastapi.purpose: Receives webhooks, exposes the console API.

g-agent.definition: A small piece of code doing one job by calling an AI model plus tools, returning structured output.

g-multimodal.definition: An AI model that understands more than text. Qwen-VL-Max can read images.

g-embedding.definition: A list of numbers capturing meaning so similar things sit close together.

g-rag.definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: fetch relevant facts and feed them to the model.

g-webhook.definition: A URL a service calls when something happens, like an inbound message.

g-hitl.definition: The system prepares the work but a person makes the final call.

chk-story.q: What is the one input a farmer provides to start a claim?
options: A photo of the damaged crop
chk-story.hint: The whole pitch collapses to one thing.
chk-story.why: A single photo (optionally with a few words in any language) is the entry point; everything else is generated.

chk-hitl.q: Where does a human still decide?
chk-hitl.why: An adjuster reviews AI assessment, weather, and fraud flags, then approves. That checkpoint is core.

chk-metric.q: What metric best captures the value?
options: Percent of eligible losses that become reviewed claims
chk-metric.hint: What failed before was filing at all.
chk-metric.why: The point is conversion: turning losses that never got filed into reviewed claims.

chk-failclosed.q: If a channel's webhook secret is unset, the webhook...
chk-failclosed.why: An unconfigured webhook is treated as closed, so a misconfiguration never opens the door to forged claims.

chk-defer.q: Where are similar-claims and fraud computed?
options: In get_claim when an adjuster opens the claim
chk-defer.hint: Who needs it: farmer or adjuster?
chk-defer.why: find_similar and fraud_check run in get_claim at review time, keeping the farmer reply fast.

chk-json.q: How is Qwen-VL output made safe to use in code?
options: Strict JSON parsed into a typed DamageAssessment
chk-json.why: A strict JSON prompt yields a typed DamageAssessment, turning the model into a reliable typed function.

chk-embed.q: How does one embedding serve both RAG and fraud?
options: Similar claims are helpful precedents and near-duplicate risk from the same search
chk-embed.why: The same similarity surfaces useful precedents and suspicious near-duplicates.

chk-sqlite.q: Why move from SQLite to Postgres in production?
options: On Function Compute the SQLite file is ephemeral and wiped on recycle
chk-sqlite.why: FC's /tmp is ephemeral, so SQLite data does not survive; Postgres via DATABASE_URL is durable.

chk-gps.q: Best way to handle the default-GPS limit in a pitch?
options: Name it as a demo shortcut with the fix (opt-in location); the pipeline already takes lat/lon
chk-gps.why: Naming the limit and its fix shows production thinking, which Track 4 rewards.

d-system.title: System map
d-system.code:
flowchart LR
  TG[Telegram] --> WH[Webhooks]
  WA[WhatsApp] --> WH
  WH --> PIPE[Agent pipeline]
  PIPE --> VL[Qwen-VL-Max]
  PIPE --> QM[Qwen-Max]
  PIPE --> OM[Open-Meteo]
  PIPE --> DV[(DashVector)]
  PIPE --> DB[(SQLite)]
  WEB[Next.js console] --> DB

d-journey.title: Photo to filed claim
d-journey.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant F as Farmer
  participant P as process_inbound
  participant VL as Qwen-VL
  participant A as Adjuster
  F->>P: photo
  P->>VL: assess_damage
  P-->>F: localized reply
  A->>A: review + fraud
  A-->>F: approved

d-data.title: Where data lives
d-data.code:
flowchart TD
  C[Claim] --> SQL[(SQLite record)]
  C --> OSS[(OSS photo + PDF)]
  C --> DV[(DashVector embedding)]

children.name: agents
children.role: one focused AI step per file

modules.title: The five-minute story
modules.summary: What ClaimFarm is, in one breath.

lessons.title: What is ClaimFarm?
lessons.summary: ClaimFarm turns a single crop photo sent over Telegram or WhatsApp into a filed crop-insurance claim.

sections.what: A chat-to-claim autopilot: AI reads the photo, checks the weather, drafts the claim, and a human approves it.
sections.why: To collapse a multi-page, multi-language, evidence-heavy form into one photo.
sections.how: A webhook runs an ordered agent pipeline that assesses, corroborates, drafts, and routes a claim to an adjuster.
sections.connects: Messaging channels in front; Qwen Cloud, Open-Meteo, DashVector, OSS in the middle; a Next.js console at the end.
sections.ifChanged: Remove the human approval and it becomes automatic payouts, which insurers reject and fraud exploits.

lessons.analogy: Like a tax app that files your whole return from one receipt photo, but an accountant still signs off.
lessons.teachBack: Explain ClaimFarm to a non-technical friend in two sentences, without saying 'AI'.

lessons.title: Why most farmers never file
lessons.summary: Around 500 million smallholder farmers face crop loss, but fewer than 20% ever file a claim.

sections.what: A conversion problem: eligible losses that never become filed claims.
sections.why: The blockers are language, the demand for structured evidence, and assumed literacy.
sections.how: ClaimFarm removes all three at the point of contact: any language in, evidence from a photo, no forms.
sections.connects: Two user types: farmers (chat) and adjusters (the console).
sections.ifChanged: If the target were a large agribusiness, a photo-first chat interface would be the wrong tool.

lessons.example: A farmer who reads only Telugu, with no scanner, produces a complete claim from a phone photo.
lessons.teachBack: Explain why '<20% file' makes this a conversion problem, not an accuracy problem.

modules.title: Architecture and journey
modules.summary: How it connects and how one request flows.

lessons.title: From a message to the backend
lessons.summary: Every channel calls a webhook on the FastAPI backend, which verifies it and runs one shared pipeline.

sections.what: A webhook front door with per-provider verification feeding one shared pipeline.
sections.why: So any messaging channel can be added without touching the agents.
sections.how: main.py verifies the caller (Twilio HMAC-SHA1, Bird HMAC-SHA256, Telegram secret) then runs process_inbound.
sections.connects: Channels in; agents and storage out; the console reads results separately.
sections.ifChanged: Skip verification and anyone could POST fake claims into the queue.

lessons.warning: An unconfigured webhook secret makes the endpoint reject requests, so a misconfiguration never opens the door.
lessons.teachBack: Explain the fail-closed webhook decision and why it matters here.

lessons.title: Inside process_inbound
lessons.summary: Follow one photo: download, detect language, assess, corroborate, draft, save, reply.

sections.what: The synchronous orchestrator that turns one photo into a saved, pending claim.
sections.why: It is the single place the whole intake sequence lives, in order.
sections.how: It base64-encodes the photo for Qwen-VL, builds a claim, renders a PDF, saves, and replies in the farmer's language.
sections.connects: Similar-claims and fraud are deferred to get_claim at review time, keeping the reply fast.
sections.ifChanged: If saving fails it still replies, prioritizing farmer experience over strict consistency.

flow.action: asks Qwen-VL for crop, cause, severity as JSON
drafts the claim and renders a PDF
replies to the farmer in their language

lessons.activity: Open app/agents/whatsapp_intake.py and list process_inbound's calls in order.

modules.title: The agent pipeline
modules.summary: What each agent does and how it stays reliable.

lessons.title: Seeing the photo
lessons.summary: Qwen-VL reads the photo with a strict JSON prompt; forensics checks the photo is authentic.

sections.what: The vision layer: a typed damage report plus an authenticity check.
sections.why: Everything downstream depends on a trustworthy read of the photo.
sections.how: A strict JSON prompt yields a DamageAssessment; forensics combines EXIF, perceptual hash, and a VL authenticity check.
sections.connects: Feeds the weather step and the adjuster's fraud view.
sections.ifChanged: Drop json_mode and downstream parsing becomes fragile.

walkthrough.code:
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """\
You are a crop insurance damage assessor.
Identify crop, cause, a severity 0-100, and confidence.
Be conservative. Return STRICT JSON only.
"""
walkthrough.note: The prompt forbids invented symptoms and demands JSON-only output.

walkthrough.code:
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
  model=s.qwen_vl_model,
  messages=[{"role":"user","content":[
    {"type":"image_url","image_url":{"url": image}},
    {"type":"text","text": user_text}]}],
)
walkthrough.note: The photo is passed as an image_url content part, the OpenAI SDK shape.

lessons.example: A stock-photo screenshot is flagged via missing EXIF and a perceptual-hash match even if the crop looks real.
lessons.teachBack: Explain how strict JSON output makes an LLM behave like a typed function.

lessons.title: Weather, RAG, and fraud
lessons.summary: Independent checks ground the AI's verdict: weather history, similar past claims, and fraud signals.

sections.what: The corroboration layer: independent checks on the AI's read.
sections.why: To ground one model's verdict in weather data and precedent.
sections.how: Open-Meteo for weather; embeddings + DashVector for similar claims; fraud is similarity-based.
sections.connects: New claims are indexed on save, so they become findable for the next farmer.
sections.ifChanged: Empty the past-claims collection and fraud-by-similarity has nothing to compare against.

lessons.insight: One embedding serves two jobs: helpful precedents for the adjuster and near-duplicate risk for fraud.

lessons.title: Drafting and speaking the language
lessons.summary: Qwen-Max drafts the claim PDF; templated, translated status messages reply to the farmer.

sections.what: The output layer: a PDF claim plus a localized human reply.
sections.why: The farmer needs an understandable response; the insurer needs a document.
sections.how: WeasyPrint renders the PDF from an HTML template; status text is translated, not free-generated.
sections.connects: The PDF is stored in OSS with signed URLs.
sections.ifChanged: Free-generating replies risks promising a payout the system cannot guarantee.

lessons.warning: Replies translate fixed templates, so the bot never accidentally promises a payout.
lessons.teachBack: Explain when ClaimFarm uses Qwen-VL vs Qwen-Max vs embeddings, one example each.

modules.title: Data, tradeoffs, mastery
modules.summary: State, honest limits, and the pitch.

lessons.title: Where data lives
lessons.summary: Three stores by shape: SQL for records, OSS for files, DashVector for embeddings.

sections.what: Three stores chosen by data shape.
sections.why: Records, files, and semantic search have different access patterns.
sections.how: SQLModel for claims, OSS for photos/PDFs, DashVector for embeddings.
sections.connects: On Function Compute the SQLite file is in ephemeral /tmp, hence the startup demo seed.
sections.ifChanged: Keep SQLite in production and data vanishes when the instance recycles.

lessons.title: Tradeoffs and the pitch
lessons.summary: Be honest about the seams. Each demo shortcut has a named production fix.

sections.what: A map of what is real, what is scoped for the demo, and the upgrade path.
sections.why: Confident, truthful framing wins judges and survives hard questions.
sections.how: Lead with the real SaaS surface and the human checkpoint; name each limit with its fix.
sections.connects: The fixes are concrete config switches (DATABASE_URL, INSURER_BASE_URL).
sections.ifChanged: Hide the limits and one pointed question can unravel the pitch.

rows.b: Opt-in location share; pipeline already takes lat/lon

lessons.teachBack: Give your 60-second judge pitch: what it is, the problem, the demo path, the human checkpoint, one honest limit + fix.

lessons.title: Teach it back
lessons.summary: Prove you can explain ClaimFarm end to end, naming real files.

sections.what: A self-assessment: can you teach the whole system?
sections.why: Explaining it confidently was the goal of this course.
sections.how: Narrate the journey, cite real files, own the tradeoffs.
sections.connects: Every prior landmark on the atlas.
sections.ifChanged: If a step feels fuzzy, revisit that landmark; the dashboard shows what needs review.

lessons.teachBack: Write your 3-minute ClaimFarm walkthrough, citing at least three real files, ending with one honest limit and its fix.
